arguments_for_no: Introducing DID functionality adds complexity to the ledger state and transaction processing, potentially increasing the risk of bugs or exploits in the new DIDSet and DIDDelete transactions.
The amendment requires validators to upgrade to rippled version 2.0.0 or later; failure to do so results in being amendment-blocked, creating operational risk for validators who are not ready.
arguments_for_yes: The DID amendment conforms to the World Wide Web Consortium (W3C) standard, promoting interoperability with broader decentralized identity ecosystems.
It provides a native, decentralized digital identity solution on the XRPL, reducing reliance on centralized authorities and enhancing user sovereignty over their identity.
The amendment has been introduced in rippled version 2.0.0, indicating it has undergone development and testing cycles prior to this voting stage.

cited_facts.why_it_matters: Defines the scope of the amendment, confirming it adds W3C-compliant DID functionality and new transaction types, which is the core value proposition for a YES vote.
Highlights the operational risk of amendment-blocking for incompatible servers, a key consideration for validator readiness and network stability.
Confirms the technical implementation is tied to rippled version 2.0.0, providing context on the software release cycle and maturity.

output_json.decision_summary: The DID amendment introduces W3C-compliant decentralized identity features to the XRPL, enhancing interoperability and user control. While it introduces new complexity and requires validator upgrades to avoid being blocked, the alignment with global standards and the benefits of native digital identity support a positive recommendation.
output_json.forbidden_stronger_claim: The DID amendment is guaranteed to be bug-free and will immediately increase XRPL adoption.
missing_evidence: Security audit results for the new DIDSet and DIDDelete transactions.
Performance benchmarks showing the impact of DID ledger entries on node synchronization times.
Community consensus metrics or validator readiness reports.
